g.title: Piper Logo, Title, and Headers
g.desc: Piper Logo, Title, and Headers

g.title: Assembled Monitor Case with Wires
g.desc: This image shows the assembled heart rate monitor from the side. You can feel two flat, 5mm plywood pieces stacked together. The top plywood piece is identifiable by the word 'PIPER' etched into its surface and four small, raised rectangular bumps on its top. Underneath this top piece, and resting on the bottom plywood piece, is a curved plastic component that holds four thin wires: black, red, white, and yellow. These wires extend out from the left side of the assembly. A thicker, flexible black cable runs over the top 'PIPER' piece, curving down the right side of the assembled case.

g.title: Front View: Sensor and Standoff Assembly
g.desc: This diagram displays a front, edge-on view of the heart rate monitor's main assembly. You will identify two long, flat, 5mm thick plywood plates: one forming the top and another the bottom of the structure. In the center, resting between these two plywood plates, is the thin, rectangular heart rate sensor, which will feel like a smooth piece of plastic or a circuit board. Four cylindrical brass standoffs, two on each side, act as pillars to hold the top and bottom plywood plates apart, creating a snug space for the sensor. You will fasten this assembly by inserting small #4-40 brass screws horizontally through holes in the plywood plates and into the threaded ends of the brass standoffs, securing the entire structure.

g.title: Kit Inventory List
g.desc: This is a list of all the parts included in your kit. Please feel and count each item to ensure you have everything needed for assembly. You should have: one flat, 5mm thick plywood 'Top' piece, and one similar 'Bottom' piece which may have different cutouts. Locate four identical 5mm thick plywood 'Side' pieces. Find two small, threaded #4-40 brass '1/2 inch Screws' and one cylindrical 'Standoff' with threaded holes. Identify two flexible rubber or silicone 'O-Rings'. You will also need one 'Sensor', which is a small, flat electronic board with metal pins, and one '4-Pin Cable' composed of flexible wires with a connector. Lastly, find two 'Velcro' pieces, one with a rough hook texture and one with a soft loop texture, that stick together.

g.title: Gather Your Kit Components
g.desc: This section helps you gather and identify all the pieces for your kit. First, find two large, flat plywood pieces: the 'Top' piece has the word 'PIPER' cut out or engraved in its center, while the 'Bottom' piece is similar in shape but smooth in the middle. Both have four rectangular slots near the corners and two smaller rectangular slots on their longer sides. Next, locate four identical plywood 'Sides' pieces, each feeling like a small animal shape with a flat bottom and a rounded end containing a circular hole. Find two small square 'Velcro' patches, one side rough and hooked, the other soft and looped. You will need two small metal 'Screws', about half an inch long, with bumpy threads and flat heads. Locate one smooth, hollow tube, which is the 'Standoff'. Also, find one flexible, circular rubber 'O-Ring', like a small, thick rubber band. Lastly, find one long, flexible '4-Pin Cable' with four wires bundled together and a rectangular plastic connector at one end, and one small, rigid, rectangular 'Sensor' component, likely plastic, which may have tiny pins or a slot.

g.title: Piper Top Piece
g.desc: This is a 5mm thick, flat plywood piece with an irregular shape. It has a wider, rounded rectangular body with a narrower, curved extension coming from one side. In the center of the wider body, feel for the laser-cut word 'PIPER'. Each letter, P-I-P-E-R, is a separate cutout. To orient the piece, position it so the 'PIPER' letters read left-to-right, with the curved extension on your left. You will find two small rectangular holes above the 'PIPER' text and two more rectangular holes directly below it.

g.title: Bottom Piece Plywood with Four Slots
g.desc: This is the 'Bottom' piece, a 5mm thick laser-cut plywood component. It feels mostly flat and rectangular. One end is narrower and smoothly rounded on both corners, like the tip of a spoon. This is the front. The piece then widens. The opposite end, the back, is wider and has two small, rounded inward curves, or 'notches', along its edge, with a flat section between them. On the wider, back half of the piece, you will feel four rectangular slots cut completely through the wood. Two slots are near one long edge and two are near the other long edge, forming a square pattern. To orient it, hold the piece so the narrower, rounded front points away from you. The wider, notched back should be closest to you, and the four rectangular slots will be on this wider half.

g.title: Plastic Side Components x 4
g.desc: You will find four identical plastic pieces. Each piece is a long, flat shape. Start from the left end; feel a rounded 'foot' extending downwards. Moving right along the bottom edge, there is a wider, shorter downward protrusion. Continue to the right end, where the piece narrows and curves upwards to a rounded tip. At this tip, you will feel a small, smooth, circular hole.

g.title: Locate Two Square Velcro Pieces
g.desc: Find two square pieces of Velcro. Each piece is approximately 3/4 inches (20 millimeters) long on each side. Unlike the smooth, rigid plywood components, these pieces feel like a soft, pliable fabric. One side of each Velcro square will feel either rough with tiny hooks or soft with tiny loops. Set both of these unique fabric squares aside with your other kit components.

g.title: Two 1/2-inch Brass #4-40 Screws
g.desc: Find two small, metallic brass screws. Each screw is about half an inch long, roughly the size of your pinky fingernail. One end has a smooth, rounded head with a single straight groove for a screwdriver. The rest of the screw is covered in fine, spiral ridges, which are the threads. These are #4-40 screws, a common small size for this kit.

g.title: One Metal Standoff
g.desc: Find one small, sturdy metal rod. Feel its sides: it has six flat surfaces or it has a smooth. Place a finger inside each end; you should feel spiral screw threads inside both openings.

g.title: Identify one rubber O-ring
g.desc: Find one rubber O-ring. It is a small, soft, and flexible black rubber ring, feeling like a thick, stretchy circular band. Identify it by its continuous smooth circular shape and elastic texture. It has no specific top or bottom orientation.

g.title: Single 4-Pin Grove to Male Cable
g.desc: This is a flat ribbon cable made of four thin wires joined side-by-side. On one end, there is a single rectangular plastic connector housing. On the other end, the ribbon splits into four separate wires, each ending in an individual thin metal pin. You can identify this piece by feeling the flat, ridged texture of the multi-wire ribbon and the contrast between the single plastic block on one end and the four loose wires on the other.

g.title: Electronic Sensor with Grove Connector
g.desc: This is a single, small rectangular electronic module. You can identify it tactually by feeling for a small, raised plastic box on one of its short ends, which is the connection port. The rest of the module is a flat rectangular circuit board.

g.title: Connect cable and mount sensor with velcro
g.desc: Identify a T-shaped wooden piece, a small rectangular sensor board with a white port on one side, a 4-pin cable with a white plug, and two small adhesive velcro squares. First, plug the cable's white connector into the white port on the edge of the sensor board. Next, peel and stick one velcro square to the flat underside of the sensor. Stick the second velcro square onto the wooden piece in the flat area adjacent to its large square cutout. Finally, press the sensor onto the wooden piece, joining the two velcro squares together.

g.title: Joining Side Pieces Using a Standoff
g.desc: Identify two plastic side pieces, which have a rounded end containing a single hole. Position them parallel to each other, mirrored so that their outer faces are oriented away from the center. Place a small, light-blue plastic cylindrical standoff between the holes of the two side pieces. Secure the assembly by inserting one brass screw through the hole of the left side piece and another through the hole of the right side piece, tightening both into opposite ends of the standoff.

text.tspan: Side pieces need to be facing

g.title: Connecting side pieces to the base plates
g.desc: This step involves two rectangular plywood plates with slots and a pair of plastic side pieces already joined by a screw. The top wooden plate, identifiable by the engraved 'PIPER' text, is already attached to the top notches of the plastic pieces. The bottom wooden plate has a small black electronic sensor with wires attached to its center. Tilt the plastic side pieces slightly to align and slide their bottom rectangular notches into the matching slots on the bottom wooden plate. Ensure the electronic sensor and its wires remain in the open space between the two plastic side pieces.

g.title: Secure the Assembly with an O-Ring
g.desc: Locate a small, stretchy black rubber O-ring. The wooden assembly has two parallel plates with small U-shaped notches on their side edges. Stretch the O-ring over the wooden assembly and settle it into these notches on both sides to hold the top and bottom plates together.

g.title: Final Assembly of the Servo Unit
g.desc: The completed assembly features a blue servo motor sandwiched between two identical laser-cut wooden plates. Each plate has a wide rectangular front and two small tabs at the rear. A black rubber band is stretched vertically around the middle of the entire stack, fitting into small notches on the sides of the plates to hold everything together. A bundle of three wires—colored red, yellow, and black—protrudes from the front of the assembly between the two wooden layers.

g.title: Continue on to make.playpiper.com
g.desc: Continue on to make.playpiper.com
